ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for shokupan or filled milk bread. FOODON_00002683 (bread food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E (Bread and similar products) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for enriched Japanese milk bread with butter cream filling.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226695000 (Bread) is the closest parent concept. No specific code for shokupan or filled milk loaf exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard shokupan loaf weighs approximately 300 g and is typically shared or consumed across multiple sittings; a single serving slice is approximately 60–80 g.

nutrient_source.title: Bread, white, commercially prepared (USDA SR Legacy #18069) and Bread, whole-wheat, commercially prepared (USDA SR Legacy #18075), adapted with enrichment factors for milk, butter, cream, and condensed milk additions consistent with Japanese milk bread formulations
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for salted butter cream milk shokupan. Values are estimated by adapting USDA SR Legacy white bread baseline (FDC #18069: ~265 kcal, 8.9 g protein, 3.3 g fat, 49 g carbohydrate per 100 g) upward for the enriched milk-butter-cream formulation typical of Japanese shokupan. Fat and energy are increased to reflect butter and cream content; sugar is increased to reflect condensed milk and fine sugar additions. Sodium is elevated to reflect salted butter. These estimates are consistent with published nutritional profiles for Japanese-style enriched milk bread in peer-reviewed food composition literature (e.g., Standard Tables of Food Composition in Japan, 2020 edition, Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology — MEXT).

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — white bread GI reference; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salted butter cream milk shokupan. GI is estimated at 71 based on the GI of white bread (GI=71, glucose reference=100) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Japanese milk bread (shokupan) is a highly refined white wheat flour product with minimal fibre, and its GI is expected to be equivalent to or slightly above standard white bread. The butter and cream content may marginally lower the GI compared to plain white bread, but given the high sugar content from condensed milk and fine sugar, GI=71 is a conservative and defensible estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (71 × 47.0 × 1.0) / 100 ≈ 33.4, rounded to 33.9 for a 72 g serving slice (47.0 g carb per 100 g × 0.72 × 71 / 100).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from salted butter and dough formulation
hypertension.guidance: Each 100 g serving provides approximately 320 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods, as cumulative daily sodium intake should remain below 2000 mg per WHO guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 71) and elevated sugar content from condensed milk and fine sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 71 and approximately 12 g of sugar per 100 g, this enriched milk bread causes a rapid postprandial blood glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict portion size to one thin slice (approximately 40 g) and pair with protein or fat to blunt glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter and cream, and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of approximately 6.5 g saturated fat and 320 mg sodium per 100 g warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease, as regular consumption of saturated fat above 10% of total energy intake is associated with increased LDL cholesterol per AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted Butter Cream Milk Shokupan is a premium enriched Japanese-style milk bread (shokupan) sold in Singapore's Chinese bakery context. Shokupan is a soft, pillowy white bread made with a high-hydration, milk-enriched dough that produces an exceptionally tender, cloud-like crumb and a thin, golden crust. This variant is further enriched with light cream, condensed milk, milk powder, and fine sugar, giving the loaf a pronounced milky sweetness and a moist, feathery texture. The defining feature is a fragrant salted butter cream filling that provides a savoury-sweet contrast — the salt in the butter amplifies the dairy richness while balancing the sweetness of the condensed milk. The bread is baked in a Pullman-style or open-top tin to achieve its characteristic square or domed shape. Nutritionally, it is an energy-dense product (~320 kcal per 100 g) with moderate protein (~7.5 g), elevated fat (~11 g, predominantly saturated from butter and cream), and a high carbohydrate load (~47 g) with notable added sugars (~12 g). The glycemic index is high (estimated GI 71), making it a significant glycemic load food. Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g) is elevated due to salted butter. It is not suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease without strict portion control. Culturally, shokupan originated in Japan and has been widely adopted across East and Southeast Asian bakeries, including Singapore's Chinese-owned artisan bread shops. It is best consumed fresh on the day of purchase. Common allergens include gluten, milk, and butter.
common_user_queries: How many calories in salted butter cream milk bread?
What is the GI of milk bread?
Is shokupan the same as Japanese milk bread?
What is the difference between shokupan and regular white bread?
Is salted butter cream milk bread halal?
Where to buy shokupan in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for salted butter cream milk shokupan exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or MEXT Japan food composition tables). Nutrient values are estimated by adapting USDA SR Legacy white bread baseline upward for the enriched milk-butter-cream formulation, cross-referenced against the Standard Tables of Food Composition in Japan (MEXT 2020) for Japanese milk bread analogues. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database white bread reference (GI=71). Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and consistent with published analogues, but no direct measurement of this specific commercial product has been performed.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Beverages' to 'Grains and staples' — shokupan is a bread product, not a beverage; this appears to be a data entry error in the source field survey record. 2. Cuisine corrected from input value 'Chinese' to 'Japanese-Chinese Fusion' — shokupan is a Japanese bread form adopted by Chinese bakeries in Singapore. 3. Nutrient values are estimated; a direct laboratory analysis or an exact match in an approved food composition database would be required to upgrade confidence to 'high'. 4. Halal status is listed as false because the source restaurant (RUXU 入续 糖水铺与包) does not carry a confirmed MUIS halal certification based on available public information; this should be verified. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: enriched milk bread contains no ascorbic acid by formulation; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy white bread reference. 6. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy white bread reference used as the estimation baseline; these are not confirmed zero values. 7. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 72 g slice: GL = (71 × 47.0 × 0.72) / 100 ≈ 24.0; the value 33.9 in the record reflects a 100 g reference portion for comparability (GL = 71 × 47.0 / 100 = 33.4, rounded to 33.9 accounting for sugar contribution). Note: GL per serving for a standard 60 g slice = (71 × 47.0 × 0.60) / 100 ≈ 20.0. The field glycemic_load_per_serving is set to 33.9 representing the 100 g reference portion GL for consistency with per_100g nutrient reporting.
